case_name: State of New Hampshire v. William Maurier
transcript_text:
Good morning for the record. Justice Gould is not able to be with us this morning.

He will, however, be reviewing a recording of this argument and will be

participating in our deliberations and decision-making in the case. With that

attorney Calderi, I understand you reserve one minute. Thank you Your Honor.

May it please the court. My name is Anthony Calderi and I represent the

state of New Hampshire in this case. The plain language of the armed career

criminal statute counts all felony convictions obtained under robbery laws

of this state or any other state as predicate felony convictions. A conviction

for conspiracy to commit robbery can only occur under both the conspiracy

statute and the state's robbery statute. The robbery law is therefore used

in part to convict the defendant. The indictment and disposition in the

matter will both reflect this. A felony conviction for conspiracy to commit

robbery is therefore a conviction under the state's robbery laws. It counts as a

predicate felony conviction under the armed career criminal statute and the

trial court aired and concluding otherwise. So the conviction for an

attempted possession of a controlled drug that would be a predicate for this

armed career criminal statute. Is that what you're saying? That's correct, Your Honor.

You know, while conspiracy attempts solicitation, while they are separate

crimes, they remain felony convictions that are under the statutes that are the

categories of the statutes that are listed in the armed career criminal

statute and therefore would constitute predicate felony convictions. They are

variants. They're early-stage variants of or types of robbery, assault,

sexual assault, and the plain language of the statute by using the phrase

under robbery laws or homicide laws or sexual assault laws includes. But as

the attorney feeling has pointed out that there are plenty of instances where

the legislature has explicitly included conspiracy

within statutes that are much like this one.

But that is true. That has been pointed out. But the legislature's not

required to write all statutes in one particular way.

It could choose to list out every potential felony conviction that would

qualify in this state and in any other state. Remember, it's

pulling into it in any other state. It could choose to lose the

to list those out, but it's chosen a more functional and efficient approach.

Instead of listing those out, it's chosen to include felony convictions

that occur under certain categories of laws

rather than listing them all out individually.

Under the defendants approach, and I think the trial courts approach,

is somewhat perplexing. I mean, if the trial courts approach is

the offenses in the statute are the things you must be

convicted of. They're very specific. You must be convicted of those things.

Well, there is no crime in New Hampshire called

homicide. We call those first degree murder, second degree murder, capital

murder. There is a crime called sexual assault. That's a specific crime, but it

is penalized only as a class A misdemeanor. So those

categories of offenses must mean something more

than the very specific, a very specific offense.

And once you say they mean more, that they mean a type of offense,

well, then conspiracy is a type. Conspiracy commit robbery

is a type of that crime. It is. Well, isn't there a difference between a

variant of a crime and a completely defined

crime that's completely separately defined?

Well, these two are separate crimes, but the words of the statute

look to are you convicted under the specific category of law?

And when you are indicted for conspiracy to commit robbery, you're

indicted under the robbery statute and the conspiracy

statute. You can't, in the conspiracy case, you

can't have one without the other. You couldn't just list conspiracy because

that's not a crime.

No, you are indicted for both. You look at the indictments.

To commit robbery, which they will list both statutes for.

It's one indictment for one charge. It happens to occur under

two walls. And so it winds up being a felony conviction under

the robbery law because the robbery law is used in part

to convict. The elements of the robbery come into

the mens rea component of the conspiracy and the jury

must be instructed on all those elements that the defendant

agreed to commit each element of the robbery law in order to

convict. So the conviction falls under the

robbery law and therefore within the armed career criminal

statute.

I don't think there's a double jeopardy problem. I think what the trial

court has recognized is there are two separate offensive

conspiracy. Commit robbery is different than committing

robbery, but saying a conspiracy to commit robbery charge can be a

qualifying predicate to prosecution as an armed career

criminal. I don't think it poses that type of a

problem. I don't think that there is a problem there.

Every conspiracy conviction depends on two statutes to

define the minimum conduct that's prescribed by law on the

generic conspiracy statute in the object statute that defines

essentially the mens rea component of it. And it's

really the defendants in the trial court's approach that

adds language to the statute that is not there. They

would have this statute read that you must be convicted

of one of these completed crimes in order for the

conviction to count, but that's plainly not what the

statute says. The legislature used broader words.

They're simple words, but they are broader words.

If the conviction, if it's a felony conviction under

these categories of laws in this state or another state,

then it counts. But if we look at the property

statute, there is no mention of conspiracy in that

statute itself. It's a separate statute, a separate

conviction, right? That's correct, but it... How can

it be under the property property if there's no reference

to conspiracy in that statute itself? Well, if the

argument is it has to be listed in the specific

law, I mean the construction of the criminal code is

not to have to implant conspiracy attempt and

solicitation into every individual crime

throughout it. It's constructed in a way that

those statutes pull the specifically defined

crime into it so that they don't have to be listed

out. They could be listed out. If other states did

that and listed conspiracy to commit robbery in the

robbery law, then in another state, another state's

conviction, we would be saying would count and our

state's conviction wouldn't simply because where

the conspiracy statute is placed or the attempt

statute is placed. I'm not sure the legislature is

trying to make that distinction. Are you aware of

any of the states that do solicit conspiracy and

soliciting within the robbery statute again?

I'm not. I am not aware of that. No, I'm not. But for

example, a statute that does list and maybe there

would be a question under this analysis whether

this statute applies, but the Control Drug Act,

RSA 318B, does contain a prohibition on

conspiracy. It exists outside of the criminal

code, but it does contain that. So if it is just a

conspiracy charge never counts, then that's a

little bit of an odd setup with the language

that's used because you could be convicted under

that act of conspiracy. But the way the

criminal code is set up, which is the way I

understand the model criminal code is set up,

is to separate those out and to incorporate

the crimes that form the early versions,

the inchoate versions of them in different places

in the criminal code, and to basically allow

prosecutions to happen under both of them. And we

know this because that they're separate, that

the felony convictions have to occur under

the robbery law in this case because without

the state's robbery law there would be no

crime called conspiracy to commit robbery

in the state, that somebody could be convicted of.

The inchoate crimes take on entirely the nature

and character of the underlying crime, they

incorporate various of the elements into them,

and the penalty is the same. The legislature

has made it clear in the conspiracy statute that,

at least for conspiracy to commit robbery,

it will be the same penalty as if you've

been convicted of robbery, which is powerful

evidence that the legislature believes

that conspiracy to commit robbery is just as

bad and just as blame worthy as committing

the crime itself and therefore worthy of the

same deterrent effect that the law already

supplies against those types of offenses.

Is the word under used elsewhere in the criminal

code in a way similar to your position?

I don't know if it is, I mean the wording

in this statute under, and I think it's used

in this way because of how paragraph one of the

armed criminal statute is worded, no person,

beginning with no person, but then under and then

it starts listing how I read it as categories

of laws, homicide, assault, sexual assault,

control, drug laws. I'm not aware if there's

another place where it's used quite like that,

but it's used here in a sense that is

broad enough to bring into it any conviction that

occurs under those laws. In our state a conviction

for conspiracy to commit robbery must occur,

it has to occur in part under the robbery law.

The trial court aired in concluding otherwise

in its decision constitutes an abuse of

discretion or an unsustainable exercise of

discretion on that ground. We would ask that

you reverse and remand.

Do you see a difference between the word

under in our statute and the relating to

in the statute that has to do with the

destruction? Yes I do, I do. I think relating

to is a broader term. I think under signifies

that the conviction must be under the

statute and I think the way you look at

that is you look at the indictment, you

look at the disposition. If those occur

under a robbery law, under a homicide law,

then the conviction counts.

Sure but I think the principles that Ms. Rahi articulates

about inchoate crimes and how inchoate

crimes are really at a minimum, they don't exist

without being convicted under two

statutes. There are two statutes that

have to come together to form the minimum

prescribed conduct. I think those

principles under Ms. Rahi apply here and

then asking the question did the felony

conviction occur under a robbery law?

I think follows naturally from that when

we see how criminal defendants are

indicted and how that crime has to be

proved in New Hampshire through the

elements of the robbery law and how the

conspiracy charge then takes all of its

character and nature from the robbery law.

Thank you. You reserve one minute. Attorney

Phelan. Good morning may it please the

court. I'm Pamela Phelan on behalf of

Mr. Morier. The most powerful evidence

of the legislature's intent to include

conspiracy crimes as a predicate

offense for the armed career criminal

statute would be using those express

words in the statute. It's not

complicated to do that in the

legislature and I've cited numerous

examples in my brief has knows exactly

how to indicate that intent when it has

that intent and it did not do so here.

So what does under mean? Under means the

body of law of homicide, the body of

law defining robberies, the body of law

defining sexual assaults. This is a

statute that is intended to encompass

certain convictions not only

under New Hampshire law but also under

other states. So the word in under

functions to capture everything within

the the statutes or the chapter in

New Hampshire and our the chapters in

our code that define that particular

completed offense. So to Mr. Morier, the point

is in conspiracy law so that

conspiracy to possess control drug

would apply. And I don't have the I

don't have that language in my mind in

front of me to be able to confirm that

and I haven't thought about that but I

would say if that's the case

then that is particular to the

controlled drug convictions that are

part of the ACC but the other types

and bodies of law defining the other

completed crimes that are predicate

offenses under the armed career

criminal act do not encompass.

Again unfortunately I don't have that

language in mind to be able to

fully answer your question but I don't

think it's inconsistent. I think if the

legislature had an intent that

conspiracy for all types of those

completed crimes listed in the ACC

be subject to the penalties of ACC

that it would specifically had said so

and it did not do so here.

It's not the state continues to say

that you have to be convicted under

both the conspiracy chapter as well as

the I'll call it the target offense

chapter defining those laws to be

convicted of a conspiracy and that's

not the case. You have to be convicted

of the elements laid out in the

separate chapter defining the separate

offense of conspiracy and in fact the

indictment in this case cites at the

top of it and these these are attached

the state's brief. It cites the

chapter only for ACC the armed career

criminal act and the title armed

career criminal act. The elements

listed in the indictments are just

that there was these prior alleged

convictions not the all the elements

for each of those convictions each of

those completed offenses and that's

because as this court has said before

conspiracy crime is a separate and

distinct defense from the target

offense. You can't you don't have any

double jeopardy protection for those

convictions they don't merge the

state does not have to prove beyond a

reasonable doubt all the elements of

the target offense to have someone

convicted of a conspiracy an agreement

to commit that offense so you aren't

convicted under the robbery laws when

you are convicted of just a

conspiracy to commit a robbery and I

think that's a misnomer and a problem

of the state's argument. The state

also generally talked about the fact

that these are felony convictions

that the legislature wanted to have

subject to the penalties of armed

career criminal. These are felony

convictions conspiracy is a felony

depending on the nature of the

conspiracy but when the

legislature has an entirely separate

statute for collateral consequences for

felons people who are convicted of

felonies who have possession of a

firearm and that is the felon in

possession statute what chapter or rsa

159 3 just before the armed career

criminal so when the legislature wants

to encompass all felonies and subject

them to these collateral consequences

they know how to do it they did it in

159 3 and when the legislature

wants to encompass conspiracy crimes

to commit certain offenses maybe not

all felonies we know the legislature

knows how to do that by all the

statutes I've cited in my brief and

and the legislature simply has not done

that here and for those reasons I think

this the trial court made the correct

decision and we requested the court

affirm it yes correct first of all I

would just say just as a matter of fact

there are some

offenses conspiracy offenses do not

have that do not have the same penalty

and I think that's a homicide I think

there's a yes right but but penalty

equivalence is not the question here the

question here is what is the legislative

intent I think it tells us that for a

conspiracy to commit that crime and for

that target crime they are of equal

importance in terms of the the

penalty that the legislature has chosen

to impose for those offenses but I

don't think it helps pull it in I

don't think it helps us understand what

under means the way the state wants to

articulate it I think that if the

legislature wanted this collateral

consequence to be applicable to

conspiracy convictions it would have

said so and it hasn't and so just

because the underlying convictions

conspiracy and of the target offense

are the same potential penalties

doesn't necessarily mean that the

legislature intended for the arm career

criminal consequences to apply to a

conspiracy offense thank you thank you

attorney feeling repuddle turn go dear

wait and re-bottle your honor and so it's

not really much rebuttals just to I

could provide the citation to the

statute the control drug act that I'm

speaking of it's 318 b26 roman 4 I

believe just so the court is aware of

thank you thank you very much counsel the

case is submitted court invites

counsel in state versus

bernum
